title: House Robber II
problem_statement: A thief is planning to rob houses along a circular street. Each house has a certain amount of money. Adjacent houses are connected, so the first and last houses are also adjacent. Return the maximum amount the thief can rob without alerting the police.

examples.explanation: Rob house 2 for 3 (cannot rob both 2 and 2 because they are adjacent in the circle).

intuition: Because the houses are circular, you cannot rob both the first and last house. Solve two linear cases: exclude the first house or exclude the last house.

approaches.name: Brute Force Circular Search
approaches.description: Try both possibilities for whether the first house is robbed, then solve the remaining linear robber problem for each case.
approaches.why_not: This is still linear time and is the natural reduction rather than an exponential brute force.

approaches.name: Memoized Linear Robber
approaches.description: Use memoization for the linear robber helper to compute the best robbery for a given range. Then take the maximum of the two valid circular cases.
approaches.why_not: Linear DP is simpler and uses constant space, but memoization shows the subproblem structure explicitly.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Linear DP
approaches.description: Compute the best robbery for two linear ranges and take the maximum. Use constant space for each linear run.
approaches.why_not: This is the standard optimal solution for circular house robber.

interview_qa.q: Why do we consider two ranges?
interview_qa.a: Because the circle prevents robbing both the first and last house, so one of them must be excluded.
Return nums[0], since there are no adjacent conflicts.

interview_qa.q: How is the linear helper function defined?
interview_qa.a: It returns the maximum rob amount for a contiguous range of houses using the standard House Robber recurrence.

interview_qa.q: Can this be done in constant space?
interview_qa.a: Yes. The linear helper only needs two variables to track the last two best values.
